label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about the letter the Indians sent to the officials from Maryland and Virginia, refusing the offer to send their boys to William and Mary College. The question asks what the Indians believed based on the letter. Looking at the options: A says they thought it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling. But in the letter, they refuse the offer and explain that their way is different. They mention that boys sent to northern colleges came back with bad manners and couldn't live in the woods. So they don't think their own way is worse. Option B says they felt insulted by the offer. The letter mentions they are thankful and not offended, though they have different views. So B is probably not correct because they thank them and don't say they're insulted. Option C claims they knew more science than the officials. The letter says they were taught all your sciences but then were useless for living in the woods. The Six Nations value practical skills in the woods over academic sciences. So maybe C is not right because they don't say they know more science, but that the other education lacks practical skills. Option D states they had a better way of educating young men. The letter says they have different ways and that their own education makes them able to live in the woods. They even offer to educate the officials' sons, making men out of them. So they believe their way is
